agent1_snippets.title: Migraine Prophylaxis -- Function -- 1. Pharmacological Therapies for Migraine Prevention
agent1_snippets.content: Various medications are available for migraine prophylaxis and prevention. Choosing the right agent is essential. The efficacy, side effects, contraindications, cost, and compliance should be considered when deciding on the right agent. It is also crucial to consider comorbid medical conditions and drug interactions. Efficacy of treatment can be assessed only after a 2 to 3-month trial; a full trial may take up to 6 months. Always start at a low dose and titrate up slowly. Monotherapy is preferred as there are no significant benefits of using more than one drug unless indicated for other comorbid conditions. Patients should be re-evaluated, and medications preferably discontinued after one year, even if they show improvement in symptoms. Successful therapy is defined as a reduction of migraine attacks by at least 50%. [3] [4] [8]

agent1_snippets.title: Migraine Headache -- Treatment / Management -- Acute or Abortive Treatments
agent1_snippets.content: Triptans should be limited to less than 10 days of use per month to avoid medication overuse. Due to their activation of 5-HT(1B) and 5-HT(1D) receptors on coronary and cerebral vessels, triptans are contraindicated in patients with ischemic stroke, ischemic heart disease, poorly-controlled hypertension, angina, pregnancy, and hemiplegic or basilar migraine. In patients with cardiovascular risks, lasmiditan, a selective serotonin 1F receptor agonist that does not induce vasoconstriction, is the preferred medication. Patients concurrently taking selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors or serotonin-noradrenaline reuptake inhibitors should be monitored for serotonin syndrome risk. Combining NSAIDs with triptans is often more effective than using either drug class alone. Initial therapy may include sumatriptan succinate 85 mg and naproxen sodium 500 mg in a single tablet. [50]

agent1_snippets.title: Posttraumatic Headache -- Treatment / Management -- Migraine-type
agent1_snippets.content: Herbal and vitamin supplements have shown some benefits in managing migraine headaches. There is evidence that magnesium, feverfew, vitamin B2, CoQ10, and Petasites can help prevent migraine headaches. [28] Triptans are the mainstay treatment of acute migraines. [29] Other abortive treatments for migraine include acetaminophen, NSAIDs, butalbital-containing medications, ergot derivatives, and antiemetics. [30] Opioids are not recommended for acute migraine therapy. [31] Monoclonal antibodies to calcitonin gene-related peptides (CGRP-mAbs) were developed to prevent migraine headaches. Other prophylactic therapies include TCAs, the anticonvulsants topiramate and valproate, and beta-blockers. [32] Botulinum toxin injection is another treatment modality for the management of chronic migraines. [33]

Although doses can escalate to 100 or 200 mg as needed, but no more than 1 dose should be administered within 24 hours. The significant adverse effect is dizziness. agent1_snippets.title: Calcitonin Gene-Related Peptide Receptor -- Administration
agent1_snippets.content: Ubrogepant is given orally at a dosage of 50 mg or 100 mg. 2 hours after the initial dose, a second dose may be taken if necessary. The dose should not exceed 200 mg in 24 hours. Treating any more than eight migraine episodes in 30 days is not recommended, as its safety has not yet been established. Like rimegepant, ubrogepant is for the acute treatment of migraine with or without aura. [16] Atogepant is given orally for the prevention of episodic or chronic migraines at doses of 10 mg, 30 mg, or 60 mg daily, with or without food. Zavegepant is given intranasally at 10 mg per spray once daily.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 891
agent2_snippets.content: Antimigraine preparations comprise a diverse group of medications used to prevent or treat migraine attacks. This category includes the oral‑dose product of clonidine, other antimigraine agents classified in the ATC system, and specific drugs such as almotriptan, naratriptan, methysergid, atogepant, dichloralphenazone, lisurid, and pizotylin. These agents vary in their mechanisms of action—triptans like almotriptan, naratriptan, and methysergid act as serotonin receptor agonists, atogepant is a CGRP receptor antagonist, dichloralphenazone provides analgesic and sedative effects, and clonidine, lisurid, and pizotylin have additional vasoconstrictive or neuromodulatory properties. Together, they offer clinicians multiple therapeutic options for individualized migraine management and improve patient care by targeting different pathophysiological pathways involved in migraine.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 68
agent2_snippets.content: Antimigraine preparations comprise a diverse group of pharmacologic agents used to prevent or treat migraine attacks. Among these agents are oral clonidine products, which are classified as antimigraine preparations, as well as other antimigraine preparations listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification system. Specific triptans such as almotriptan, naratriptan, and the zolmitriptan 2.5 mg oral tablet are also members of this class, and the zolmitriptan tablet contains the inactive ingredient hypromelóza. Hypromelóza, besides serving as an excipient in zolmitriptan, is linked to nasal vasoconstrictor formulations, reflecting its broader relationship to vasoconstrictive agents used in the nasal cavity. Additional antimigraine drugs include atogepant, dichloralphenazone, flunarizin‑dihydrochloride, lisurid, methysergid, and pizotylin, all of which are recognized as types of antimigraine preparations. The category further encompasses a sub‑class of alkaloid compounds referred to as “námelové alkaloidy,” highlighting the chemical diversity within migraine therapy. Together, these agents illustrate the wide range of mechanisms—ranging from serotonin receptor agonism to CGRP antagonism and vasoconstriction—employed to manage migraine, underscoring the importance of selecting appropriate formulations and excipients for optimal patient care.
